data.data:
[EVIDENCE]: [2026-03-09] Somar Meteorologia reported Minas Gerais received 14.9 mm of rain last week, only 35% of the historical average. [2026-03-03] The NOAA/CPC GFS model suggests a pessimistic outlook for March and April with below-average rainfall. [2026-03-03] Late-February heavy rains caused severe flooding and 72 fatalities in Minas Gerais. [2026-03-05] Conab projects a record 2026 Brazil coffee harvest of 66.2 million bags (+17.2% YoY) due to favorable moisture. [2026-03-10] Coffee prices fell to 293.74 USd/Lbs as markets react to the bumper crop.
[ANALYSIS]: The previous analysis correctly identified the crop stage and cited specific sources (Somar, NOAA, Conab), but it exhibited a slight confirmation bias by dismissing the potential negative impacts of recent weather extremes. While it noted the transition from floods to dry weather, it glossed over the risk that severe flooding (72 fatalities) could cause localized crop or infrastructure damage, and that a prolonged dry spell in March/April (as forecast by NOAA) could still negatively impact final bean size during the maturation phase. However, the overarching bearish sentiment is highly justified by the data. Conab's projection of a record 66.2 million bag harvest (+17.2% YoY) is the dominant market driver, actively pushing prices down to 293.74 USd/Lbs. The market is currently prioritizing the massive supply influx over localized weather volatility, maintaining a strong bearish outlook, though the emerging dry spell warrants close monitoring for late-stage quality degradation.
[CONFIDENCE]: 0.8
[SENTIMENT TAG]: [SENTIMENT: BEARISH]

[EVIDENCE]: 1) [2026-03-10] USD/BRL fell to 5.2049 from a March 5 peak of 5.282. 2) [2026-03-09] End-2026 USD/BRL forecast is 5.41. 3) [2026-01-29] Selic rate maintained at 15.00%, with 2026 forecast raised to 12.13%. 4) RAW RESEARCH: Recent Real recovery from 5.28 may cause producers to delay sales waiting for dollar strength.
[ANALYSIS]: The previous analysis suffered from recency bias by overemphasizing the short-term FX fluctuation while underestimating the severe holding costs imposed by a 15.00% Selic rate. While the recent strengthening of the Brazilian Real (USD/BRL dropping to 5.2049) reduces immediate local currency revenue for USD-denominated coffee exports, the highly restrictive 15.00% interest rate makes financing inventory prohibitively expensive. Although raw research suggests farmers may temporarily withhold physical supply, their capacity to hold out is severely constrained by these high borrowing costs. Furthermore, the projected weakening of the BRL to 5.41 by end-2026 indicates that any delayed selling will eventually hit the market, capping long-term price upside. Therefore, the competing forces of unfavorable short-term exchange rates (discouraging sales) and punitive interest rates (forcing sales) result in a more balanced, neutral outlook for Arabica prices.
[CONFIDENCE]: 0.65
[SENTIMENT TAG]: [SENTIMENT: NEUTRAL]

[EVIDENCE]: [2026-03-09] MSC suspended all cargo shipments to the Middle East following military strikes on Iran; Maersk and CMA CGM also halted Red Sea transits. (Produce Report) | [2026-03-08] Rerouting around the Cape of Good Hope has become the 'new normal,' adding 10-15 days to transit times. (DHL Kenya) | [2026-02-19] Port of Santos handled a record 12.7 million tonnes in January 2026, driven by a 79.6% surge in soy complex exports. (DatamarNews) | [2026-01-13] EUDR compliance deadlines are officially moved to Dec 30, 2026 (large firms) and June 30, 2027 (small firms). (IntegrityNext)
[ANALYSIS]: Recent logistical data indicates compounding supply chain constraints that tighten available supply. The previous analysis suffered from false equivalence by conflating DHL Kenya's (Mar 8) assessment of Cape rerouting as the 'new normal' with the acute, subsequent shock reported by Produce Report (Mar 9): MSC suspending all Middle East cargo and major liners halting Red Sea transits entirely due to military strikes. This represents a fresh, unpriced systemic disruption rather than a stabilized baseline. At origin, DatamarNews (Feb 19) highlights a 79.6% surge in soy exports at the Port of Santos. This creates intense localized competition for container availability and port infrastructure, threatening coffee export flows. Although IntegrityNext (Jan 13) confirms the EUDR compliance delay removes the immediate demand-side panic for European front-loading, the severe physical restrictions on supply movement—both at Brazil's primary port and through major global shipping lanes—outweigh the eased regulatory pressure. The combination of acute shipping halts and origin port congestion justifies a bullish sentiment.
[CONFIDENCE]: 0.8
[SENTIMENT TAG]: [SENTIMENT: BULLISH]

[EVIDENCE]: [2026-03-10] The Containerized Freight Index maintained a 17.58% increase over the past month, trading at 1489.19 points (Trading Economics). [2026-03-04] Strait of Hormuz passage halted due to conflict, increasing shipping insurance costs for coffee producers and forcing reroutes via the Cape of Good Hope (Perfect Daily Grind). [2026-02-19] Cecafé reported January 2026 export volumes dropped 30.8% to 2.780 million bags (Cecafé). Raw research indicates approximately 55% of vessels at major Brazilian ports like Santos face delays due to outdated infrastructure and high flow volumes.
[ANALYSIS]: A critique of the previous analysis reveals a false causation fallacy: it assumed the 30.8% drop in January exports was solely caused by port congestion, ignoring other potential factors like harvest cycles or inventory levels. It also failed to explicitly name the sources in the text and confusingly stated a 'bullish outlook for physical Arabica availability'—constrained supply is bullish for price, but means availability is low (bearish). REVISED ANALYSIS: Logistics and export data indicate compounding supply chain constraints that are bullish for coffee prices. Cecafé reports a 30.8% drop in January 2026 export volumes. While multifactorial, this reduction is severely exacerbated by physical bottlenecks, with raw research showing 55% of vessels at key Brazilian ports like Santos facing delays. Furthermore, global freight costs are surging. Trading Economics notes a 17.58% monthly rise in the Containerized Freight Index, while Perfect Daily Grind reports that the Strait of Hormuz conflict is forcing costly reroutes via the Cape of Good Hope and driving up insurance premiums. The combination of reduced export flow, persistent port congestion, and escalating shipping costs creates strong upward pressure on global Arabica prices.
[CONFIDENCE]: 0.8
[SENTIMENT TAG]: [SENTIMENT: BULLISH]

[EVIDENCE]: 1. ICE-monitored arabica inventories rose to a 5-month high of 553,379 bags on March 9, 2026, up from 528,028 bags on March 4 (Barchart, CocoaIntel). 2. The coffee futures forward curve remains 'clearly backwardated' (TradingView). 3. Brazil's Conab projects a record 2026/27 harvest of 66.2 million bags, a 17.2% YoY increase (Trading Economics). 4. Global coffee exports from Oct 2025-Jan 2026 increased 7.5% to 46.38 million bags (ICO).
[ANALYSIS]: A critique of the previous analysis reveals a significant hallucination/factual error: the inclusion of 'Strait of Hormuz shipping disruptions,' which is irrelevant to major coffee trade routes and was entirely unsubstantiated by the provided evidence. Furthermore, the previous analysis suffered from false equivalence by weighting the backwardated curve equally against overwhelming supply data. The revised analysis corrects this: While a backwardated forward curve traditionally signals near-term physical tightness (bullish), this structure is directly contradicted by the bearish reality of rising ICE certified stocks, a 7.5% increase in global exports, and a projected record Brazilian harvest of 66.2 million bags. Because physical inventories are actively building, the backwardation is likely a lagging pricing anomaly that will soon flatten or flip to contango as the record harvest reaches the market. The sheer volume of incoming supply dictates a bearish macroeconomic outlook.
[CONFIDENCE]: 0.65
[SENTIMENT TAG]: [SENTIMENT: BEARISH]

[EVIDENCE]: 1. As of March 3, 2026, non-commercial traders held a net long position of 10,800 contracts in Coffee C futures, with longs at 47,704 and shorts at 36,904 (source: Tradingster / Barchart). 2. Speculative positioning reached the 1st percentile (Z-score -2.34), indicating extreme low net length and 'positioning washout' (source: cotdata.uk). 3. Week-over-week, longs decreased by 617 contracts and shorts increased by 1,397, confirming a bearish shift in speculative sentiment (source: CFTC COT report via MarketBulls). 4. On March 9, 2026, NY Arabica coffee prices hit a three-week high amid low rainfall (35% of average) in Brazil's Minas Gerais and shipping threats in the Strait of Hormuz (source: Barchart). 5. Brazil's 2026/27 coffee crop is projected at a record 70.6 million bags, potentially offsetting supply concerns (source: USDA FAS).
[ANALYSIS]: The original analysis exhibits mild confirmation bias by emphasizing bullish supply tightness while underplaying countervailing factors like Brazil's record crop projection, which could cap upside; no strong recency bias, as it balances recent price action with positioning data, but potential false causation in linking low speculative longs directly to short-covering rallies without confirming volume or catalyst strength. Sources were specific and cited appropriately, avoiding vagueness. The bullish sentiment tag is partially justified by the price rebound and fundamentals but overstated given the extreme low positioning signaling weak demand; overall, this creates a neutral-to-bullish setup with risks of reversal if crop yields exceed expectations. Improved by incorporating crop data for balance, the divergence suggests short-term upside from fundamentals but medium-term caution due to supply abundance.
[CONFIDENCE]: 0.65
[SENTIMENT TAG]: [SENTIMENT: NEUTRAL]

[EVIDENCE]: [2026-03-09] The 200-day Simple Moving Average (SMA) for Coffee Mar '26 futures is $389.20. (source: TradingView); [2026-03-09] Coffee Mar '26 (KCH26) closed at 301.75, marking a new 3-week high with a daily gain of 1.23%. (source: Barchart); [2026-03-09] Immediate technical resistance levels are identified at 303.97 (R1) and 306.18 (R2), while support levels are at 299.57 (S1) and 297.38 (S2). (source: Barchart); [2026-02-21] Bullish divergence was observed in RSI and Stochastic indicators as price tested the 280 support zone. (source: TradingView); [2026-03-09] Somar Meteorologia reported that Brazil's Minas Gerais coffee-growing area received 14.9 mm of rain last week, just 35% of the historical average. (source: Barchart)
[ANALYSIS]: The KCH26 contract's close at 301.75 remains significantly below the 200-day SMA of 389.20, confirming a bearish long-term trend driven by excess supply in the Arabica coffee market. The 1.23% daily gain and 3-week high indicate short-term bullish momentum, corroborated by RSI and Stochastic divergences near the 280 support in late February, suggesting potential oversold conditions easing. Support levels at 299.57 (S1) and 297.38 (S2) offer downside protection, while resistance at 303.97 (R1) and 306.18 (R2) caps near-term upside. Subpar rainfall in Brazil's Minas Gerais (35% of average) raises valid supply risk concerns that could support prices if yields are impacted, but this weather event alone does not establish causation for a reversal without broader data on crop health or global demand; the long-term bearish structure persists absent sustained volume and breaks above resistance.
[CONFIDENCE]: 0.65
[SENTIMENT TAG]: [SENTIMENT: NEUTRAL]

[EVIDENCE]: Arabica futures (front-month) fell 1.12% to 293.74 USd/Lbs, pressured by a projected record 2026/27 Brazilian harvest of 66.2 million bags. KCH27 settled at 274.95, highlighting a backwardated curve driven by near-term Middle East logistical risks. Implied volatility is extremely elevated at the 89th percentile compared to realized volatility at the 48th percentile. Open interest is rising as futures test support near 280 cents/lb, elevating near-term squeeze risk, which is corroborated by a pronounced bid for out-of-the-money (OTM) options.
[ANALYSIS]: The previous analysis correctly identified the high volatility regime and the fundamental divergence between near-term logistical tightness and long-term Brazilian oversupply. However, it failed to adequately weigh the immediate squeeze risk indicated by rising open interest and the pronounced bid for OTM options, which could easily blow through short-term resistance. The market's backwardation (front-month at 293.74 vs. KCH27 at 274.95) demonstrates that the record 66.2 million bag Brazilian harvest is already heavily discounting deferred contracts. While the macro trend for KCH27 remains structurally bearish due to this massive incoming supply, the acute near-term squeeze risk makes undefined-risk bearish strategies highly dangerous. A defined-risk strategy, such as a Bear Call Spread on KCH27, remains the optimal approach. It capitalizes on the rich implied volatility premium (89th percentile IV vs. 48th percentile RV) and aligns with the bearish long-term supply fundamentals, while strictly capping upside exposure in the event that Middle East logistical disruptions trigger a violent short-covering rally.
[CONFIDENCE]: 0.65
[SENTIMENT TAG]: [SENTIMENT: BEARISH]
